label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what made Woolworth a world-famous man. The options are A to D. Let me read through the passage again to pick out the key points. First, the passage starts by talking about his background. He was born into a poor farming family and wanted to avoid farming. He took a business course and became a salesman. He had a natural skill for displaying goods to attract customers. The example given is when he sold odds and ends at five cents each, which was successful. He opened his own store, but learned that he needed to buy in bulk directly from factories. The example of the knives in Germany where he ordered so much that the factory had to run 24/7 for a year, cutting the price in half. By 1919, he had many stores and became famous. The key rule was "The customer is always right." Now, the question is what made him world-famous. The options are: A. His business skills and his wealth B. The low price of the goods he sold C. His trip to Germany and his huge order of knives D. His natural skill for displaying things Looking at the passage, he became famous due to his business success, which included both his skills and the wealth he accumulated. The trip to Germany and the knife order was an example of how he applied his business strategy, but that's one specific instance. The low prices were part of his strategy, but the main reason he became famous would be his overall business acumen and the success of his stores, leading to his wealth and the widespread recognition of his name. Option A combines both his business skills and the resulting wealth, which seems to be the broader factors leading to his fame.
